nativeRange.habitat: Backwaters, floodplains, and tributaries of the upper Amazon basin and Araguaia River; typically slow-moving or still water with dense vegetation

decorPreferences: dense planting along sides and back

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates, insects, larvae, and crustaceans. In aquaria, generally accepts a wide range of foods including quality flakes and micro-granules, freeze-dried and frozen bloodworms, Daphnia, brine shrimp, and tubifex.

summary: Active, peaceful schooling tetra from the Amazon basin, known for its distinctive oblique black stripe and head-up swimming posture. Generally considered a good beginner fish when kept in groups of 6 or more.
careNotes: Best kept in groups of at least 6 (10+ preferred) — fin-nipping behavior is significantly reduced in properly-sized schools. Tends to swim in a distinctive head-up, oblique angle, often near the top of the tank. Closely related to Thayeria obliqua (Hockey Stick Tetra), which has a shorter black stripe not reaching the lower caudal lobe — the two are sometimes confused in the trade. Sensitive to elevated ammonia, nitrite, and nitrate despite tolerating a fairly wide pH range. Prefers densely planted aquaria with open swimming lanes; adding Indian almond leaves or dried botanicals can help recreate the natural tannin-stained environment. Does not require a tightly sealed lid under normal conditions.
breedingNotes: Reported to breed fairly readily in captivity. A dedicated spawning tank with soft, slightly acidic water and a mesh liner or spawning mop is generally advised. Females may produce several hundred to up to several thousand eggs per spawn; parents have been known to eat eggs and should be removed after spawning. Eggs tend to hatch within 12–24 hours; fry typically become free-swimming within 3–4 days. Initial foods: infusoria or commercial fry powder, graduating to microworms and brine shrimp nauplii.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary; Aquarium Co-Op (designated primary for fish-freshwater slice) has no dedicated Penguin Tetra care page.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (3.6 cm SL / 6 cm TL), temperature (22–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–19 dH), and native range. All high-stakes fields confirmed consistent with SeriouslyFish.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Additional cross-check. Temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 5–20 dKH, size 3.8–7.6 cm, tank 20–30 gal (76–114 L), lifespan 3–5 years, min group 6. Also noted fin-nipping tendency in small groups.
